item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely fantastic. 200 IQ scip. The idea is great in itself, imo, the added epistemological dread is just a bonus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm embarrassed I didn't recognize "εὕρηκα" and had to have Google explain it to me.
It's "eureka" in Greek, fyi.

I'm left wondering, can the proof be independently derived?
What I mean is, can you feed any well-educated physicist/philosopher who is unaware of 1714 or skips in general the starting points; and they always come to the same reality-bending conclusion?

Because if not, if the only way to come to this conclusion is to have seen Dr. Molly's work first, that is the difference between science-not-yet-understood and a memetic hazard created by a fledgling Type Green that incidentally propagates the Green "species".

As written, it could be read either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … she assumed a spherical cow so hard that it became true? Wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of an evil version of http://www.scp-wiki.net/kate-mctiriss-s-proposal

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑This right here so much I wish I could upvote comments because if the SCP Foundation existed & I saw some proof I would avoid them at all fucking cost like him and also be kinda happy that non-normal stuff exists out there but also depressed considering that they don't want this stuff to be known about and they're so huge that they're practically impossible to take down except maybe with SCP-868.

Lol well that was a run on sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me a lot of that skip that was a large mass growing out some dude's head, and contained net involved actively working out the equation while maintaining suspension of disbelief,, or something along those lines.
(If someone could point me back to it, that'd be swell. Need me a favorite button and a list.)

+1, stuff like this makes for the best headaches.

item.title: NOOOO
item.content:encoded: God damn it! I had this exact idea, so I searched 'SCP maths problem' into google and this came up! Have an upvote anyway

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This bears another read through, as enjoyed my first. I love hard science skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, the problem arises from the fact that while we might judge something's anomalousness by whether it violates the laws of reality, the Foundation judges whether something is anomalous by whether they think civilization could take it.

Like, say you read in the paper today that there are gaping exceptions to what you've been told your whole life are ironclad laws, exceptions so big that one person exploiting them properly could fuck up an entire solar system. Supposedly these are 100% consistent and understandable, but when you know that reality as you know it can just sort of splinter and fuck up the things you care about, wouldn't you be at least a little disconcerted? Distrusting of the people who told you that there weren't gaps this big right in your own backyard? Wondering why exactly you're not seeing many of these ex-anomalies?

The Foundation could absolutely try and study this to create more effective containment procedures, but 1714 still requires containment, is anomalous, and is an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gotta agree with this. Assuming this document is true and it's really describing a fundamental law of the universe that the Foundation simply does not fully understand yet, then it's not anomalous. Or at least it's no more anomalous than Einstein's theory of relativity would have been to Isaac Newton. Instead of containing it the Foundation should be trying to finish it. I'd think they would love to be able to scientifically explain roughly half their SCPs and use the theory to create new more effective containment procedures.

Yeah the real SCP is definitely the crazy, solar system destroying doctor and she's definitely keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, having last read one of the first editions on your sandbox, this took me entirely by surprise. Well written, interesting to read, easily +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I was a bit disappointed by the lack of any quotations from the maths part, and the reasonably clichéd 'disturbed scientist' but the story drew me in and I'm a sucker for massive stellar events which are light years away. Take a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat one, nice and tidy. Nicely put together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read the description and was scratching my head over what, exactly, was anomalous about it. (That it proves anomalies to be non-anomalous?) But it seems both like A) this is technically a skip that (can allow someone to) creates other skips, and B) the skip isn't really the proof so much as the Doctor and her newfound godlike powers that she seems all too eager to test.

And yet I found the story compelling enough for an upvote. A good, if slightly weird article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading through this again, I noticed that the observation of the system occurred ██ years after the disappearance, and the system was ██ light years away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is solid, with a good back-story and well written with some humor, so +1.

I think it should definitely be Euclid, regardless of Addendum 02. The Foundation has to constantly monitor the world's academia as part of the containment and that's quite an effort.

Now I wonder: is she a scientist who has figured out some underlying principles of anomalies, or a reality bender who is using science as her "crutch"?

That's also something I wondered about. There is nothing in the story that shows that the Math proof itself has any actual use (just because it seems logical does not mean it's correct) and it may not even be anomalous at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oooh! Very clever of you to have noticed! It would be pretty stupid of me to forget something as fundamental as that. One explanation is that the anomaly was propagated a few months or years after Dr. J's initial disappearance and it was only recently noticed.

But if you look carefully at the [DATA EXPUNGED]… you can draw your own conclusions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed a small problem, how would the foundation be able to observe that other solar system changing from light years away? They would be seeing the system as it was years before. Of course, time travel might have been involved

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent job. A good mix of science and wonder. Now I wonder: is she a scientist who has figured out some underlying principles of anomalies, or a reality bender who is using science as her "crutch"? Either way, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of curiosity, why are only d-class allowed to read it? I might need to read it again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you everyone for the warm reception! I'm really happy with how this turned out and I want to thank everyone who provided input and advice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was wondering if I should make it Keter. At first it was, but the concept was sort of different so that the skip was pretty well contained. What does everyone else think? I suppose it's not really contained anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept was cool, and the I enjoyed the way you executed it. Indeed, a very nice piece. +1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think my favorite part in this is seeing a view of the Foundation from the view of someone who doesn't know they exist, but is smart enough to figure some things out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a big fan of the "hard science" type articles on the site, and this really fulfilled my expectations for those articles. Which is tricky, because it's very easy to veer into "dry and uninteresting" territory when attempting these. I like the story, I like the effort put into this, and I like the ultimate scope of what you're doing here. Upvoted enthusiastically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really do not like the core concept—to me, it almost seems like…anti-horror, or anti-interesting. It is the direct opposite of stuff that excites me on this site. The story also did not really grab me, given that the professor seems to be very clear-cut "psychotic mad scientist with apocalyptic goals 'justified' by psychosis," which doesn't appeal to me as interesting. I was not a fan of the expungement in the last note either. It is well-written, though. so yeah -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still like this, and you fixed the issues I had with it in chat. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did not come in expecting to enjoy an "explain all/many anomalies" SCP, but you managed to make that work with a good story. Solid. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cannot math very well, but I still understood what the general gist of this is. And I think it's neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fundamental theory so elegant that it actually turns the Universe into things like point masses moving along geometrically idealized paths? SOLD. Take this upvote, and well done on a good first effort.
